figures.caption: Figure 1: Top: Simulated steady states at t = 1000 for control settings v, p ∈ [0, 1]. Red triangles indicate training configurations v, p = 0.1 + 0.1i for i = 0, . . . , 8. Bottom: 21 training trajectories for the configuration p = 0.5 and v = 0.25. Vertical line denotes training cutoff, t = 200.

Figure 18: Feedback control loss landscape for various control configurations. Black crosses denote true minima found and red crosses denote minima found using gθ via solving corresponding objective: 1 2∥x− gθ(x,u)∥2. A coarse grid search and subsequent root finding scheme (scipy.optimize.root) was used to determine solutions for both the true and learned model.

Figure 17: Comparison of true versus learned simulated trajectories: using full fθ(x)(x − gθ(x,u)). Initial conditions are sampled uniformly from x(0) ∈ [0, 6]2, while control parameters (α1, α2, β, γ) are drawn uniformly from [0, 5]4.

Figure 19: Top: Control restricted feedback control experiment for genetic toggle switch system eq. (38) for 10 randomized targets every t = 20 perturbed by noise with scale σ = 0.05. Bottom: Continuous control with control iteration and strength are given by k = 1 and η = 1 as defined in eq. (10) with constraints given by eq. (39).

Figure 5: Simulated trajectories (with t on a logscale) for model fθ(x)(x − gθ(x, λ)) (blue dashed line) trained on data up to t = 0.25 (red dashed line) and true dynamics given by eq. (33) (black solid lines) for control values λ = [−1,−0.55,−0.45,−0.4,−0.35, 0.35, 0.4, 0.45, 0.55, 1] up to t = 100 with bistable cases highlighted in red.

Figure 10: Comparison of effect of η for continuous control from eq. (8) for unstable target, x = 0.2, traversing tipping points, λ = ±2/ √ 27, of symmetric hysteresis loop. The effect of control strength, η, from an underdamped control to sufficiently strong control to mitigate the effects of tipping point (left to right).

Figure 15: Comparison of true and modeled bistability landscapes across varying cooperativity levels. Left: Overlay of bistability regions (“Arnold tongues”) in the (α1, α2) parameter space for symmetric cooperativity cases (β = γ). The wedge shapes delineate the bistable (memory) regime from the monostable regimes. The dashed horizontal line at α2 = 1.5 indicates the cross-section used for the bifurcation analysis on the right. Right: Steady-state response of x1 (top) and x2 (bottom) as a function of α1, with α2 fixed at 1.5. Solid lines represent the true system dynamics, while dashed lines represent the learned model predictions using gθ. The colors correspond to the cooperativity levels defined in the left panel.

Figure 4: Stochastic feedback control experiments with 10 randomized targets reassigned every t = 500. Top: Itô simulations with state-proportional noise of scale σ = 0.01. gθ for both tanks 1 and 2 plotted, where control iteration and strength are k = 10 and η = 0.1, respectively. Bottom: Constrained continuous feedback control solving eq. (10) jointly with the stochastic system where control constraint is given by eq. (32).

Figure 3: Absolute error, for tanks 1 and 2, between model and true steady states for control values p, v ∈ [0, 1]. Model steady states are obtained from solving associated root finding problem, x = gθ(x,u). True steady states obtained from solving system numerically at t = 1000.

Figure 12: Learned bifurcation diagram from solving associated root finding problem r(x) = x − gθ(x, κ) for κ ∈ [4.44, 11.93]. Stability of each steady state is determined by evaluating ∂ ∂x [f(x)(x − g(x, κ))]x=x∗ . Stable states are denoted in blue, unstable in red. Dashed lines denote learned model, black solid lines denote true, numerically simulated results. Gray dashed vertical lines denote system tipping points.

Figure 11: Comparison of the true and learned zero-level sets across x ∈ [0, 10] and κ ∈ [4.44, 11.93]. Left: The true zero-level set (black solid line) of x−g(x, κ). Right: The zero-level set (black solid line) of the learned dynamics x− gθ(x, κ). Black dashed lines denote the system tipping points at κ = 6.446 and 9.934.
Figure 8: All steady states learned from solving root finding problem r(x) = x−gθ(x, λ) for λ ∈ [−1, 1]. Gray vertical lines indicate system tipping points.

Figure 9: Top: Continuous feedback control for 10 randomized targets uniformly chosen from [−1.5, 1.5] where implicit equilibria function gθ(x, λ) included to show the behavior around tipping point. The states were perturbed by noise with scale σ = 0.03. Bottom: Continuous control with control iteration and strength given by k = 1 and η = 5, as defined in eq. (8). Grey dashed horizontal lines denote tipping points of system.

Figure 2: Six trajectories with initial conditions x(0) = (0.1, 0.1), (0.9, 0.2), (0.2, 0.9), (0.1, 0.6), (0.6, 0.1), and (0.8, 0.8) for 25 control configurations plotted on true phase portraits generated from eq. (30) (black line) compared against model simulated trajectories from fθ(x)(x − gθ(x,u)) (blue dashed line). True and model steady states are denoted by black and red crosses, respectively.

Figure 16: Left: Histograms of the learned function fθ evaluated over a dense grid where x1, x2 ∈ [0, 5]. Black vertical dashed lines indicate mean values of −0.9224 and −0.8152, with standard deviations 2.720 · 10−2 and 6.807 · 10−2, respectively. Right: Output of the learned control function gθ on the same domain for increasing control parameters α1 = α2 = β = γ ∈ {1, 3, 5}. The top and bottom rows display the first and second output dimensions of gθ, respectively. Notably, the learned dynamics exhibit univariate behavior.

Figure 13: Top: Continuous feedback control for the Budworm ODE given 10 randomized targets perturbed by noise with scale σ = 0.02. Bottom: Continuous control with control iteration and strength are given by k = 1 and η = 20, respectively, as defined in eq. (8). Grey dashed horizontal lines denote tipping points of system, κ = 6.45, 9.93.

Figure 6: Left: Learned fθ compared against f from eq. (34) across training regime x ∈ [−2, 2]. Right: Learned gθ compared against g in eq. (34) for various control values λ. Red highlighted control indicates bistable regime, λ ∈ [−2/ √ 27, 2/ √ 27], that is 3 steady states occur, 2 stable and 1 unstable–denoted by the intersections with the diagonal gray dashed line x = x.

Figure 7: Comparison of the true and learned function outputs across the state and control space. Left: Output of x− g(x, λ) (from eq. (34)). Right: Output of the learned approximation x− gθ(x, λ). In both panels, the zero-level set is denoted by a solid black line, and the black dashed lines indicate the system tipping points at λ = ±2/ √ 27.
